channel.title: AI Campaign News (AICN)
channel.description: A tracker of AI use in political campaigns, elections, and advocacy.

item.title: Campaigns Need AI Guardrails Before the 2026 Midterms
item.description: A Campaigns & Elections op-ed argues that the political conversation about AI in elections has been too narrowly focused on deepfakes and synthetic media, and calls on campaigns to adopt broader AI guardrails — covering AI-driven targeting, messaging, and voter contact — before the 2026 midterms.

item.title: Tech: Democrats have AI committee hopes too
item.description: Punchbowl News reports that several House Democrats are calling for the creation of a formal bipartisan AI policy committee, mirroring an effort Republicans declined to institutionalize, as Congress continues to grapple with how to organize legislative oversight of artificial intelligence.

item.title: Tech: How Husted and his voters see AI
item.description: Punchbowl News reports that Sen. Jon Husted (R-Ohio) says constituent concern about AI has broadened beyond parents worried about children online to include employers and educators, suggesting AI anxiety is becoming a wider voter issue heading into 2026.

item.title: The Startup Betting on an AI Advertising Tool For Politics
item.description: Campaigns & Elections profiles BattlegroundAI, a startup that has launched an AI tool enabling campaigns to instantly generate platform-compliant programmatic political ads with minimal manual input.

item.title: AI safety group's $15 million GOP play
item.description: Public First Action, an AI safety advocacy group, announced it will spend $15 million in July 2026 to run ads highlighting 16 congressional Republicans for their work on artificial intelligence safety, turning AI policy into an explicit electoral issue.

item.title: AI Moves From Campaign Tool to Campaign Issue
item.description: Governing reports that AI is now reshaping 2026 campaigns on two fronts simultaneously: campaigns are deploying AI operationally, while AI policy itself has emerged as a voter-facing issue candidates must address.

item.title: Tech: Cruz approaches AI's make-or-break month
item.description: Punchbowl News reports that Senate Commerce Committee Chairman Ted Cruz faces a make-or-break window of several weeks to move federal AI legislation forward, with the alternative being a delay until the 120th Congress.

item.title: The First AI Midterms: Early Signals from California
item.description: Higher Ground Labs published a debrief of AI tool use across California's June 2026 primary — covering the governor's race, the Los Angeles mayoral contest, and down-ballot races — framing the cycle as the first major stress test of AI in American electoral politics. The piece draws early operational lessons for campaigns deploying AI in the 2026 midterms.

item.title: Sanders-backed progressives try to make AI the new AIPAC
item.description: Semafor reports that Sanders-aligned progressive organizers are working to build a grassroots political movement opposing AI data center expansion, drawing an explicit parallel to single-issue advocacy campaigns, with New York Gov. Kathy Hochul's data center moratorium cited as evidence the effort is gaining mainstream political traction.

item.title: Data center backlash puts candidates in governor races in a bind
item.description: The Hill reports that data center proliferation — driven by AI demand — has become a divisive issue in multiple 2026 gubernatorial races, forcing candidates to navigate constituent concerns about energy use, land use, and the pace of AI expansion.

item.title: DOGE Used AI for Housing Policy. The Government Won’t Say How
item.description: Wired reports that DOGE used AI tools to shape federal housing policy but the Department of Housing and Urban Development has withheld documents about that use in response to a public records request, in part by citing a legal privilege that Wired says does not exist.

item.title: NO FAKES Act gains Senate Judiciary Committee approval
item.description: The Senate Judiciary Committee approved the NO FAKES Act, advancing the bill — which would protect individuals against unauthorized AI-generated digital replicas — to the full Senate for consideration. The Recording Academy has expressed support for the legislation.

item.title: Getting campaign text messages ahead of midterms? There could be an AI bot behind it
item.description: NPR reports that campaigns are using AI bots to send and manage political text messages to voters ahead of the 2026 midterms, with the practice spreading across multiple campaign operations. The piece examines how automated conversational AI is replacing or supplementing human texters in voter contact programs.

item.title: 21-year-old AI startup cofounder's sales pitch to clients: 'Do not trust us'
item.description: Fortune profiles Aaru cofounder Ned Koh, 21, whose AI startup sells synthetic-respondent predictions to clients as an alternative to traditional polling, with Koh himself warning clients not to take the results on faith while arguing the platform's predictive accuracy surpasses human survey data. The piece examines Aaru's unusual sales posture and its growing footprint in political forecasting.

item.title: Aaru AI Review 2026: The Prediction Startup, and Where It Fits
item.description: An independent review from FishDog assesses Aaru's AI prediction platform — which raised a Series A and is run by a teenage founder — examining where its synthetic-respondent methodology is credible and where it falls short relative to conventional polling. The review highlights both the startup's technical ambitions and the limits of its current validation record.

item.title: Announcing Higher Ground Labs' Agentic AI Open Call
item.description: Higher Ground Labs, a progressive political technology incubator, has opened an investment application specifically for agentic AI startups building tools for political use, signaling institutional progressive-side investment interest in autonomous AI agents for campaigns. The open call invites founders to pitch agentic solutions across campaign functions.

item.title: Tech: Lawmakers want agencies watching AI election threats
item.description: Reps. Mike Lawler (R-N.Y.) and Josh Gottheimer (D-N.J.) are urging four federal agencies to take action on potential election-integrity threats posed by AI ahead of the 2026 midterms.

item.title: Bipartisan lawmakers press agencies on AI election threats
item.description: The Hill reports on the same bipartisan push by Lawler and Gottheimer urging federal agencies to monitor AI-related election threats in the 2026 cycle.

item.title: What AI companies want for the millions they're spending on elections
item.description: CNBC reports that two major AI industry super PACs are each spending heavily in the 2026 midterms and lobbying Congress for competing versions of AI regulation, illustrating how industry money is shaping the legislative landscape.

item.title: Getty Images’ Answer to AI Slop? Authentic, Verified Content
item.description: Getty Images is marketing authenticated, human-verified visual content to political campaigns as a direct counter to the spread of AI-generated imagery, framing provenance and verification as competitive advantages in the 2026 cycle.

item.title: NO FAKES, Real Censorship: How Congress Could Muzzle AI Speech
item.description: The Cato Institute argues that the NO FAKES Act of 2026 would create sweeping liability for anyone who displays, distributes, or transmits an unauthorized AI-generated likeness of a person, or who builds a tool capable of producing one, effectively criminalizing a broad swath of expressive and journalistic activity.

item.title: AI Legislative Update: July 3, 2026
item.description: The Transparency Coalition's July 3 legislative roundup reports that California Governor Gavin Newsom signed a law requiring public school teachers to be human, while New Jersey legislators passed a Kids Code Act, illustrating continued state-level AI governance activity.

item.title: Tech: OpenAI warms up to Washington
item.description: Punchbowl News reports that OpenAI has backed Sen. Marsha Blackburn's Kids Online Safety Act and the NO FAKES Act, signaling a warmer relationship between the company and Washington lawmakers heading into the 2026 cycle.

item.title: 'Who should I vote for?' Voters turn to AI before casting their ballots
item.description: The Philadelphia Inquirer reports that voters are using AI chatbots to research candidates and guide their voting decisions ahead of the 2026 midterms, even though the tools are designed to avoid expressing political opinions.

item.title: 30 States Now Have Laws to Regulate Election Deepfakes
item.description: Public Citizen reports that 30 states have now enacted laws regulating AI-generated deepfakes in election communications, with Maryland Governor Wes Moore's signature on a new law marking the most recent addition to that count.

item.title: Tech: What unions want on AI policy
item.description: Punchbowl News reports that labor union leaders, framing AI-driven automation as an existential threat, are planning aggressive engagement in Washington AI policy fights and in the 2026 midterm elections.

item.title: Gavin Newsom treads the AI line
item.description: Semafor reports that California Governor Gavin Newsom is walking a careful line between pushing to regulate the power of major tech companies while simultaneously using AI products in his own political operations.

item.title: Get Ready To See a Lot More AI in Campaign Ads
item.description: The Dispatch surveys the growing use of AI in campaign advertising, finding that while some campaign staffers are embracing AI to amplify messaging, others are raising ethical questions about the strategy, with deepfakes specifically mentioned as a concern.

item.title: Donald Trump's Improvised AI Regime
item.description: The Dispatch characterizes the Trump administration's approach to AI governance as improvised and ad hoc rather than a coherent regulatory strategy, raising questions about how AI policy will be set heading into the 2026 elections.

item.title: Anthropic Added a New Security Measure to Get Back Into the Trump Administration’s Good Graces
item.description: Following a prior freeze, the Trump administration removed export restrictions on Anthropic's Fable 5 and Mythos 5 AI models after Anthropic added a new security measure, marking a partial repair of the strained relationship between the company and the White House.
